CURRENT AFFAIRS.

It turns out now that the Orange bluff is to blossom
out, in September, into a sort of ananimous resolution,
but as unanimous resolutions are somcwhat provoca
tive of smiles in modern enlightened Ireland, ‘the
Orange bluffers are calling it a ‘‘ covenant.”  The Car-
sonites are to pledge themselves against three things,
one of which unhappily is not strong drink: they ure,
if & Home Rule Bill becomes law, not to acknowledge
the Parliament in Dublin, not to obey its laws, and
not to pay taxes to it. As the taxes are to be cgl-
lected in the early years by officers of the Impetial
Parliament, that aspect of the unanimous resolution
‘to be let off in September need not immediately worry
anyone. If Dunville’s whiskey dries up rather than
pay duby, so much the better for competing whiskies.
These Orange playboys are becoming as burlesque as
the Shin Feigners of a few years #2o v
end and generaligsimo, our genial friend and harmlese
rebrand, Mr. John Sweetman, replied that ’nobw!th-

ugo when their then.

standing all his Shin Feignery he paid his Income Tax
as if he did not his furniture would b: seized. The
Shin Feigners were to cut down, if not wipe out, their
consumption of taxed commodities, and minute calcu-
lations were made as to what the foul taxgatherer
grabbed out of an ordinary cup of tea. We hear vo-
thing of that now, even though at the time ‘a certain
number of foolish and feather-headed people discussed
the matter with grave countenances. If Belfast tobacco
manufacturers shut down rather than pay duty, the
tobacco manufacturers of ‘‘ the South and West >’ may
look out for a large increase in their trade. Orange drum-
ming parties may at last become comparatively re-
spectable parades if the drummers have the pledge
against taxes. But the Carsonites will have a respite
so long as the Imperial officials zollect the taxes, for
the terms of the proposed resolution leave a loophole.
There will be a greater difficulty in not acknowledging
the Parliament in Dublin, for that would seem to pre
clude any of them taking a paid job in the gift of that
Parliament! It is a wonder that a hunger-strike 1s not
one of their proposals. Even the dullest Briton, nay,
even some of the wooden musketeers of County Down,
must be beginning to see the farce of all this silly play
acting.

An alleged Home Ruler named Douglas, assistant
editor of the London ‘‘ Star,”” happens to be an Ulster
Protestant, and his game is to make the” most of the
‘‘ grim "’ Orangemen. The fact that a proposal which
his Home Ruler publishes is made the text of a lead-
ing article in the ‘‘ Irish Times " iudicates the sort of
Home Ruler that he is. This man Dougles published
a letter he received from someone whom he says ‘s a
Protestant Home Ruler, and this lette: contains the
suggestion that the Liberals should drop the Home
Rule Bill, allow things to simmer for about ten years,
and then the Conservatives could bring in a better
Home Rule Bill. Another letter to this man Douglas,
his time from a Protestant TUnionist, expresses,
amongst other things, the view that if Home Rule
were carried, no Catholic employe would be tolerated
in any mill or factory in Belfast. Well, things don’t
appear to be too rosy even now in that direction in the
city of *“ civil and religious liberty.”” But why all the
wreats on one side? Suppose that the Liberals did
abandon Home Rule, or supposing How. Rule for any
other reason is not passed, is there no kick left in the
Nationalists 2 Can they do nothing? Well, we will
wait and see.

Charming Tame Clonmel hes had a band promenade
in connection with its Rowing Club. ~ And ‘‘ Tommy
played the music—for a consideration The Rowing
Club of tame Clonmel comprises local traders, a few
professional men, some shop assistants, and a few bank
clerks.  Recently tame Clonmel sen! two crews to
compete at Cork and Limerick regattas, and both crews
brought home prizes to the tame little town on the
Suir. Tame Clonmel became wild with delight and it
organised a public reception for the returning heroes.
Great crowds, headed by the three local bands, meb
the conquerors at the station, and (l_le latter were
received through the decorated town with enthusiasm
and band ‘music. < We have not heard that any of the
three mere local bands was paid : when ﬁherg was pay-
ing to be done the playin% was given ovar to “Tommy.
The local bands were allowed to voluntarily turn out
to welcome the Rowing Club men, but: when. the Row-
ing Club were on for hiring a band th> choice fell on
To-amy. :

Cookstawn in the County of Tyrone has had its Horti-
cultural and Agricultural Show. A circular touting for
emigrants for Canada, where the frost and snow are,
was distributed to nearly everyone going to the Show,
but particularly to servant boys and girt ~ We suppose
il a Show were held in Canada and somccne attempted
to distribute circulars calculated to induce ignorant
people to leave Canada he would be hunted cff the ground.
Of course there are some people that this -7 uptry would
be well rid of, and if Canada induced the Leifast black-
guards to emigrate we would not be sorry. But Tyrone
wants its servant boys and girls. This Cookstown Show
is & rather Unionist affair. The committee and officials
are all, or nearly all, composed of people who are “saved,”
and the Carsonites, various non-Catholic bishops and
others are identifying as far as they can the cause of
Unionism with the non-Catholics. Catholics, we hear,
have a poor chance of winning any prize at this Show.
Since the Unionist club was started in Cookstown Catholics
do not appear to be eligible even to act as gatemen, as
the gatemen at 5s. for the job are recruited from the
Unionist clubs. Last year some ‘ idolaters * ‘were em-
ployed at the entrance at 4s. for the day, but this year

'no Papist need apply. There was only one Catholic
judge, and out of the various prizewinners only about
six were Papists.

Avcusr 81, 1919,

A éorrespondent from England writes us :— {

Sir,—I beg to propose that on the 29th day of
Sept. next the people in every town and parish of
Nationalist Ircland shall meet together and by
solomn league and covenant pledge themselves to
have no business relations whatever with Belfast—:
to buy no goods coming from Belfast, to touch np
.merchandisc coming through Belfast and to. dis-
courage any intercourse whatever with that town,
I further propose that your esteemed contributor
« Columecille ” organisc a committee -to take this
matter at once in hand.

It is a very natural if not an altogether wise suggestion,
TFor one thing it would be taking the Carsonites too
seriously. There are many estimable people and firms
in Belfast, and we would not like to suggest the mere
possession of such a thing as say the Belfast accent
should be a handicap to a man. Belfast has a very
considerable Nationalist population, and you cannot
indiscriminately hit Belfast without hitting Nationalists
and other respectable citizens. By all accounts some
Belfast travellers in the maligned “ South and West ”
are being made feel that the ““ South and West ” has
at least as much natural resentment as a worm. The
suggestion of our correspondent is useful as an indication
of how- feeling is rising against Belfast atrocities,
and against the cowards in Belfast and all over
the country,. who, whilst they profit on National-
ist trade, have not the manhood to denounce
the atrocities. The Carsonites and their forthcoming
unanimous resolutions are rather ridiculous, and we
would not agree that it would be wise for the Nationalists
of every town and parish of Ireland to resolute against
Belfast goods and goods coming through Belfast on the
day after the politico-sectarian farce on September 28th :
in any case we would be against indiscriminate opposition
to Belfast. But it is well to show that Nationalists can
hit back and be “ grim ” well as the braves of the
shipyards. Bellast has earned an ugly and a dirty name.
in Ireland, and it is wholesome that Belfast bigots should
be taught that fact to their faces and to their tills. They
have prevented inoffensive Catholic Irish people from
earning their Why, then, should bigots be helped
to fortune by the rest of Ireland when places other than
bigot dens in Belfast can supply on equal terms the needs
of the much maligned ““ South and West 2 It is all
very well to ery “We will not have Home Rule,” but
then we can be goaded into crying ““ We will not have
Belfast bigots.”

‘We have seen that in a firm in Ballyclare, Co. Antrim
all the Catholics—and they were not many—were cleared
out. We have not heard that any of that sort of work,
was tried on at the paper mills at Ballyclare. As
a maftter of fact we don’t know how many, if any,

THE TAME GEESE.
A STORY OF 1914.
’Twas in the year that Home Rule became law,
And alien ascendancy gave its last caw,
That Ireland at length saw the end of the story
Of Ned of the Hills and lus traitors so gory.
This Ned of the Hills was a bloodthirsty chief,
A feather-bed soldier who fought with a brief,
And hating Home Rule, at the head of a faction,
Against that great measurc he swore to take action.
So when old John Bull, pretty tired of the past,
Declared he’d deal justly with Ireland at last,
Up jumped my bold Carson, as if with a writ,
And swore his clodhoppers would never snhmit.
His armchair anarchist flag he unrolled,
And under it flocked all the lambs of the fold,
Led on by the Crai the Jim Crows, and the Laws,
And all the old Dic-hards of Unicnist caws.
Then there ensued preparations so stern, oh,
That Belfast for Papists became an inferno.
Then started the training of sentries and scouts,
And drilling of higotry’s ignorant louts,
Inspired and instructed by elderly blades,
Of asinine majors and army old maids.
Who dreamt of upsetting all notions Home-ruling
By goose-stepping bluster and rascally” fooling.
. Oh! ’twas a fine stage-managed bogey affair,
This thunder and turf{ Orange Unionist scare.
But the game didn’t prasper, for Ireland at last
Found out how to deal with the scum of Belfast ;
For bigotry’s traders soon tiré of its rockets
© When Romanist customers hit at their pockets.
Some stuck to their colours, some cut away clean,
While crafty Alf Fox oscillated between ;
And so to the end amidst loyal decamping
They kept till Home Rule put an end to their tramping.
Now, Carson, remember your traitorous tallk,
And.lead on your ras this-measure to balk ;
Remember your threats-every law to pull down.
And hurl defiance at Kingdom and Crown.
Yow: place-hunting lawyer, remember your vow,
Set up your provisional government now,
And ‘backed by cach rowdy and Unionist wrecker,
Refuse to pay taxes to Ireland’s Exchequer.
But Carson’ the spouter ol treason so stale

THF LEADER.

e 59

Stopped short at the course that would land him in jail.
The thought of a warrant or'prison sent chills
Into this ignoble poor Ned of the Hills.
This desperate Die-hard of Parliament lobby
Now shivered with fear at the sight of a bobby.
He gazed upon Craig, and he looked at Jim Crow,
‘And found, like his own, that their courage was low.
Te gazed upon all and their faces expressed
The braggart and craven revealed in the test;
And finding his gang just the same as himsellf,
All out for no fighting but lucre and pell,
He showed the white feather and said to his mob,
““Ned Carson, my boys, only fights for a job,
So right-about-turn each Carsonite tool
And grab what you can under Irish Home Rule.”
So ended the tale of this breaker of peace,
This Ned of the Hills who led on his tame geese.
A. M. W.

The Keating Branch of the Gaelic League will hold
their annual Aeridheacht at the Thatch, Drumcondra,
on Sunday, 1st September. Songs will be contributed
by leading Dublin vocalists. ~Dancing, musical solos,
and recitations will also be important features, whilst
two_pipers’ bands will do much to enliven the pro-
ceedings, which will commence at 8.30. Most of the

;rtlst.es have won prizes at the Oireachtas and Dublin
fels.

An ‘“Irish Irelander” living in Belfast writes:—.
“‘ Reading the par. in last week’s LEADER re the dearth
of Trish papers in England, I could not help thinking
of a personal experience of not a month ago.” Return-
ing from a Continental ramble, where I had been
deprived of a sight of Irish affairs, I turned into the
busy bookstall at Victoria Station, London, for an
Irish paper.  Of course, the LEADER was my first
request. ‘ No, we do not keep it.” ‘ Any other Irish
paper, then.” ‘We keep none, unless on order,” was
the careless, indifferent reply. And so on through
all the great London newspaper shops, till T arrived
at the Irish Stores in Victoria Street, where at last I
got my LEapER. Just contrast this incident with what
happens at Dublin or Belfast each Sunday morning.
The incoming boats cannot discharge their putrid
papers quick enough.  Messengers from every °airt
the wind may blow * are eagerly waiting their quotas.
The early summer morning sees no one abouf, save
and except. the English Sunday paper vendor, and
decent newsvendors have assured me that out of count-
less dozens sold on Sunday morning they could safely
say four copies was the limit of the sales in the
Catholic papers. When will our people assert them-
selves?  They are quite content to follow the low
standard set them by these penny-a-liners in English
newspaper offices, and then shout ‘ Ireland a nation!*
when they are English in thought.”

60.

. There is ample time between this and September
80th for the country to demonstrate to the *“ Liberal ”
Mr. Samuel that it will not have Norway as Secretary
of the Trish Post Office. When a Mr. W. J. Evans was
appointed Chief Inspector for Wales under the Insur-
ance Act, Welsh protests were made because Mr. Evans,
though otherwise apparently exceptionally well quali-
fied for the position, did not speak Welsh. And so
Mr. Eyans resigned, and the ‘“ Western Mail "’ of Car-
diff said:—*‘ The Commissioners will have to make
another appointment, and they will appoint a Welsh-
speaking Welshman.”  Is Ireland to put up with the
Norway dump in face of the fact that Wales made
things so hot that even a Welshman-—we presume from
his name that Mr. Evans is a Welshman—who did not
speak Welsh would not be accepted by gritty little
Taffy as Chief Inspector? The appointment had to
be changed because Wales objected, and this Norway
appointment will have to be changed because Ireland
objects.

Though theve is yet ample time, there is no time to be
lost in expressing Ireland’s determination that it will
not have Norway. We are pleased to see that Lime-
rick is stirring, and the people who gave Ireland a lead
by making a bonfire of imported ‘‘ Penny Dirties *’ are
in their right place in taking a leading part in the *“ We
will not have Norway '’ movement. Too long we have
been putting up tamely with the tap of the Orange
drum; it is time to tap the Irish drum, and this Nor-
way insult is a convenient and a concrete opportunity
to enable Ireland to show that she is determined to
fight her corner and not take so many tricks, insults
and swindles lying down.

Our Limerick contemporary, the ‘‘Limerick Leader,”
devotes its leading article of August 23rd to what it
calls ““ The Threatened Dump.’”’ In the course of its
article it says:

““ There are already evidences enough that Eng-
land’s policy of dump will not be silently and
tamely acquiesced in on this occasion as it has
so often and so foolishly been in the past. The
public representative bodies are taking the matter
up, and many of them have voiced the feelings of
the people in strongly-worded resolutions in con-
demnation of the threatened importation. The
Dublin Corporation and the Dublin County Coun-
cil were the first bodies to take action on the ques-
tion. The Limerick County Council, alert and

patriotic body that it is, has also raised an em-
phatic voice against the proposed dump.

THE LEA™ ER.

Avecusr 31, 1912,

last meeting it passed a resolution * assuring the
Postmaster-General that such an appointment
would cause intense disappointment in view of the
provision in the Home Rule ]3’.“] for the transfer
of the Post Office to the Irish Government.”
Amongst the other bodies that have entered pro-
tests is the Croom District Council, which yester-
day unanimously adopted the resolution of the
Timerick County Council. It behoves every
public Board and Council in Ireland to take
up this matter and raise a storm of indignant and
vehement protest and condemnation that will pre-
vent the infliction of what could be re_garded as
nothing but a positive and galling injustice. T]:le
importation of an Englishman to the secretaryship
of the Trish Post Office implies the suggestion tha
there is no Irishman fit for the position. Ts Tre-
land to lie low under such an insult?, If she does
she certainly deserves more kicks than respect
from her foreign rulers. The announcement that
the appointment is to be for a period of only two
years is intended simply to throw dust in the eyes
of the public. ~As Mr. John Coleman rightly re-
marked at the meeting of the Croom District Coun-
cil on yesterday, ‘‘ making it a temporary appoint-
ment for two years is a greater insult than the
appointment itself. It shows that during that
time they could not trust an Irish official.” = In a
vigorous and useful article on the question the
Dublin LeaDER, a paper thap has done splendid
work in the crusade for Irish rights, says: “ If it
were honestly intended to dump this man over here
for two years the dump remains quite as objec-
tionable, whilst the act increases in silliness; and,
needless to say, the two years’ business only in-
creases our suspicions that an unserupulous job is
on hand.”” Apart from its injustice the importa-
tion would be about as illogical a piece of business
as a Government could well carry out.  Liberal
Ministers bring in a Home Rule Bill, one of the
clauses of which states that the Irish Post Office is
to be handed over to Irish control. If these states-
men believe Treland is fitted to take up the whole
management of the Post Office how is it one of
their number can be under the impression that
Trelapd has not a man competent to fill the Secre-
taryship?  The kernel of the situation, of course,
is this—that a lucrative position is vacant, or about:
to become vacant in Ireland, and that those having
the filling of it in their hands think it a pity to give
it to an Irishman while an Englishman is avail-
able. Ireland has something to say on the matter,
and it should say it with no uncertain voice, and
give the Government to understand that, as our
plucky contemporary, the LEADER, puts it, ‘‘ Ire-
land does not want Norway."”
We hope that other provincial papers will follow the
example of the ‘‘ Limerick Leader,” and do their part

When the Gaels were returning thz plous censors -
gave them keen attention. We quote from the report

NEW ENGLISH-IRISH DICTIONARY.*

UDENTS and teachers alike will welcome the
publication, just at the opening of the new school
year, of Father Dinneen’s English-Irish Dictionary.
It is a handsome volume, nicely bound in cloth, and
published at one shilling. 1t is thus within the reach
of every student, and so it is certain to facilitate
immensely the objects of the Gaelic movement.

The volume bears the same impress of thoroughness
and completeness that characterises all that comes
from the hand of Father Dinneen.  His method of
arranging and classifying his mafter is, as usual, lucid
and masterly. The hook is a delight to work with,
clear-typed, concise, free from vagueness. It has not, it
is true, the bulk and proportion of the author’s larger
Irish-English Dictionary.  But this is in the nature
of things, for the book subserves a different purpose.
It places an efficient tool for wielding the language
into the hands of those who, for want of such an aid,
have often found themselves forced to fall back on
the Béarla.

We are not of those who would prohibit to learners
the use of an English-Irish dictionary. On the con-
trary, we find that many of even the most eminent
students cannot make the language their own without
such an aid to their work. There are, in our schools,
colleges and Gaelic League branches, numbers of intel-
ligent students whose knowledge of the grammar and
construction of Irish is quite sound, but who are often
withheld from using the tongue they love, as the
language of their correspondence, because they are not
sure of the spelling or inflection of some word. Having
deyeloped in the course of their studies a sense for

or at least it is not written to please Irish readers. Why
we write about the book at all is that so many Irish

Wwittingly, if they do not positively caricature Irish life,
at least show us up in a very bad light. Unfortunately
those who know Tvish life thoroughly cannot or will not
write about if, and those-who don't know it, or are not
l'lt‘ a position to know it, will insist upon writing about
it.

We do not know whether Mr. Colum lived for any
length of time in those parts of Ireland he describes—
the Midlands and Connaught—Dbut his description lacks
spontaneity and that confidence which a man who
knows his subject always possesses. He goes about
from place to place evidently busy with his note-bool,
and he gives the views of various persons on various
topics. This may appear to the reader as first hand
information that cannot be gainsaid, but in reality it is
misleading; it is not the views of any individual mem-
ber of a class that the stranger or the reader should get,
but rather an opinion that will echo the general views
of the body. The same holds good of the description
of any member of a class of persons; it is not an in-
dividual, perhaps abnormal, member that should be
described, but an average type of the class, otherwise
a false impression is created. It is on these points
that Mr. Colum fails; he does not know his subject
thoroughly, and depends too much upon hearsay, and
the reader is seriously led astray.

Mr. Colum, in the early portion of his book, makes
the startling statement that *‘ almost everybody in Ire-
land is badly fed.”” Of course, that is an absurd asser-
tion to begin with. Speaking of the peasant he says
he has ‘‘ white bread and tea for breakfast; potatoes,
cabbage and foreign bacon for dinner, and tea again and
again.”’ This is not ideal fare, no doubt, and though
we are not ready to concede that the use of milk, eggs
and butter has gone out—we know it has not—we may
ask Mr. Colum if he has ever travelled on the continent
and seen the daily fare of the French, Italian, or Swiss

- peasant. Clearly he has not.

Speaking of the Irish mind in Irish literature Mr.
Colum quotes, with approval, Mr. George Moore’s
““ Hail and Farewell ”’ and Mr. Synge's ¢ Playboy of
the Western World.””. Mr. Moore’s book is, according
to our author, ‘‘ distinctive of the.Irish spirit ! Every
reader of the LEaDER has good reason to know what a
sickening caricature of Irish life the ‘“ Playboy " is,
and we might quote from a reviewer in the *‘ Ivernian
Journal ”” what class of book is ‘“ Hail and Farewell ’:

and' a nature morally debased. In little asides scattered
through its pages the author does not scruple to show
himself & man devoid of faith and morals. He is a son
of one who in his day was a good Irishman and a good
Catholic.  Consideration for his father’s memory, for
the faith in which he was nurtured, and for the land
that gave him birth should have some conftrol cver
him. It has not. He takes a mean delight in pour-
ing slimy contempt over what ordinary people regard
with reverence. Ivery man of clean mind would wish
that Mr. Moore would betake himself to the purlieus of
the French capital, where he may possibly receive due
encouragement in his literary work from comrades of a
like mind with himself.”’

In the introduction Mr. Colum tells us very demon-
stratively that he is an ‘‘Irish Catholic.” ~We sus-
pected at once that there would be something forth-
coming that would account for this very unnecessary
piece of information, and we were not disappointed. The
average reader never makes a mistake about diagnosing
the writings of a Catholic, but he will receive a rude
shock if he always expects the same class of matter
rom a man who styles himself an ‘‘ Irish Catholic.”
His favourite theme is Church building, and the clergy
—not the Protestant clergy, they appear never to
trouble him—but the priests, there is always some-
thing wrong with them! He invariably reads them a
ittle lecture, not to give offence, of course, but for
their good, and for the good of the country.

Mr. Colum is no exception to the literary ‘‘ Irish
Catholic ’* that we meet so often in these days, and
he has his little lecture in Chapter IX., which he styles
the ‘‘ agrarian priest.”

He tells us all about the collection at a funeral where
Father Michael receives £5 17s. 6d. as an ‘‘ offering,”’
and then he tells us that this is a relic of penal times—
but does not forget to note that it is ‘‘ galling to fami-
ies,”” and suggests that it is a pity that the offerings
are not transferred from burials to marriages. A well
informed man like Mr. Colum ought to know that this
custom of offerings at funerals is only confined to a
small part of Treland, no doubt ‘‘ coming down from
the penal times,”” and that his suggestion has always
been acted upon in the rest of Ireland, namely, that
there are offerings at marriages. We are sure if Mr.
Colum were living in those parts where this is the
custom—** coming down, of course, from the penal
times "'—he would very probably pronounce this as an
equally * galling” custom. In other countries where
these galling customs do not exist the faithful pay a
substantial offering at Mass; in Ireland a penny or a
